summary: Charity Majors reflects on the power of team rituals to create belonging, drawing from a book on workplace rituals and her own extensive experience at Linden Lab and Parse/Facebook. She argues that the best rituals emerge bottoms-up from teams rather than top-down from executives, and that they disproportionately contribute to the most meaningful workplace memories.
key_points: Rituals create a powerful sense of belonging by linking physical actions with psychological and emotional meaning
The best engineering team rituals emerge bottoms-up from peers and line managers, not from executives
Rituals normalize failure and spread knowledge organically, as with the Shrek Ears tradition for first production incidents
Celebrating painful work like tech debt cleanup reinforces that unglamorous contributions matter
Great rituals often make no sense to outsiders, and that exclusivity is part of what creates belonging
money_quotes: That ineffable sense of belonging can make all the difference between a job that you do and a role that feeds your soul.
Congratulations on breaking production — you're really one of us now!
A ritual that comes from your VP can run the risk of feeling like forced fun, in a way it doesn't if it's coming from your peer or even your manager.
Great rituals often make no sense to anybody who isn't in the know — that's part of the magic of belonging.
Coincidentally (or not), I have no memories of participating in any rituals at the jobs I didn't like, only the jobs I loved.
key_insight: Engineering team rituals that emerge organically from the team itself create a powerful sense of belonging that transforms a job into a meaningful experience, and the best ones normalize failure, celebrate unglamorous work, and make no sense to outsiders.
